question: [File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit an Authorization where authorization.authorized == address(ecrecoverAuthorizer) and isAuthorized == false, revoking the ecrecoverAuthorizer's permission to act on behalf of the authorizer, and does this create a state where previously submitted (but not yet mined) authorizations from ecrecoverAuthorizer will fail, causing a DoS on pending authorization transactions? Preconditions: authorizer has authorized ecrecoverAuthorizer; pending authorization transactions exist in mempool. Call sequence: attacker front-runs with ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized({authorized: ecrecoverAuthorizer, isAuthorized: false}) -> pending txs fail. Invariant tested: revocation of ecrecoverAuthorizer must not be exploitable for DoS. Scoped impact: fund freeze / DoS on authorization flow. Proof idea: simulation test with front-running revocation.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: Midnight.setDefaultSettlementFee + Midnight.setMarketSettlementFee] Can the feeSetter set defaultSettlementFee[loanToken][i] to value X, then create a market (which inherits X), then set marketSettlementFee(id, i) to Y, then set defaultSettlementFee[loanToken][i] to Z, resulting in three different fee values (X in history, Y in market, Z in default) with no invariant enforcing consistency between them? Preconditions: feeSetter controls all fee parameters. Call sequence: setDefaultSettlementFee(token, i, X) -> touchMarket -> setMarketSettlementFee(id, i, Y) -> setDefaultSettlementFee(token, i, Z). Invariant tested: market fees and default fees must be independently valid but their divergence must be bounded. Scoped impact: fee inconsistency enabling unexpected taker costs. Proof idea: state-transition test asserting all three values are independently stored and valid.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker exploit the fact that EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized is callable by anyone (msg.sender is not checked) to submit a valid Authorization that grants the attacker authorization over the authorizer's Midnight account, if the attacker can obtain a valid signature (e.g., via social engineering or a compromised signing key)? Preconditions: attacker has obtained a valid signed Authorization. Call sequence: attacker calls ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth, sig) from any address. Invariant tested: permissionless submission must require valid signature; no caller-based bypass. Scoped impact: gate bypass if signature is obtained. Proof idea: unit test asserting any caller can submit valid auth; assert invalid sig reverts.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: Midnight.touchMarket + Midnight.setDefaultContinuousFee] Can an attacker create a market at a time when defaultContinuousFee[loanToken] == MAX_CONTINUOUS_FEE, and then the feeSetter sets defaultContinuousFee to 0, causing the existing market to have MAX_CONTINUOUS_FEE while new markets for the same token have 0, and does this create an exploitable inconsistency where lenders in the high-fee market are disadvantaged compared to lenders in the zero-fee market? Preconditions: feeSetter sets defaultContinuousFee to MAX; market A created; feeSetter sets default to 0; market B created. Call sequence: setDefaultContinuousFee(token, MAX) -> touchMarket(marketA) -> setDefaultContinuousFee(token, 0) -> touchMarket(marketB) -> compare lender returns. Invariant tested: per-market continuousFee is fixed at creation; lenders must be able to predict their fee obligations. Scoped impact: fee inconsistency between markets; lender fund loss in high-fee market. Proof idea: differential test comparing lender credit after identical positions in market A vs B.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker construct a malformed EIP-712 signature (e.g., with v=27 and crafted r,s values) that ecrecover returns a non-zero address that happens to equal authorization.authorizer or an authorized delegate, bypassing the signature check? Preconditions: attacker knows the target authorizer address; crafts (v, r, s) such that ecrecover returns authorizer. Call sequence: ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth, malformedSig) -> ecrecover returns authorizer -> signer == authorization.authorizer -> passes. Invariant tested: ecrecover must not be forgeable; signature verification must be cryptographically sound. Scoped impact: gate bypass via signature forgery. Proof idea: fuzz over (v, r, s) values; assert only valid signatures pass; note ecrecover malleability with v in {27, 28}.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: Midnight.setMarketTickSpacing] Can the tickSpacingSetter reduce tick spacing from DEFAULT_TICK_SPACING=4 to 1 on a created market, enabling all ticks to be used, and does this create a state inconsistency where the market's effective price granularity changes mid-life, potentially enabling offers at ticks that were previously inaccessible and that makers had not intended to be fillable? Preconditions: market created with tickSpacing=4; offers exist at tick=2 (inaccessible); tickSpacingSetter reduces to 1. Call sequence: touchMarket -> setMarketTickSpacing(id, 1) -> take offer at tick=2. Invariant tested: tick spacing reduction must not retroactively enable unintended offer fills. Scoped impact: offer bug; maker intent violated. Proof idea: state-transition test asserting tick=2 offer becomes fillable after spacing reduction to 1.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized + Midnight.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker use EcrecoverAuthorizer to grant authorization to a contract that, when called as an authorized agent, re-enters EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized with a new Authorization (using the next nonce), creating a reentrancy loop that grants multiple authorizations in a single transaction? Preconditions: authorizer has authorized ecrecoverAuthorizer; attacker deploys a reentrant contract; authorizer signs auth granting reentrant contract authorization. Call sequence: ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth1) -> midnight.setIsAuthorized(reentrantContract, true, authorizer) -> [no callback in EcrecoverAuthorizer, so no reentrancy path]. Invariant tested: EcrecoverAuthorizer must not be reentrant. Scoped impact: multiple authorization grants in single transaction. Proof idea: verify EcrecoverAuthorizer has no external calls after nonce increment that could enable reentrancy.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: Midnight.setMarketSettlementFee] Can the feeSetter set settlementFeeCbp0 (0-day breakpoint) to a non-zero value, causing post-maturity settlement fees to be non-zero (since post-maturity uses the 0d breakpoint), and does this create a state inconsistency where lenders expected zero post-maturity fees but face non-zero fees? Preconditions: market created with settlementFeeCbp0 = 0; feeSetter calls setMarketSettlementFee(id, 0, MAX_SETTLEMENT_FEE_0_DAYS). Call sequence: touchMarket -> setMarketSettlementFee(id, 0, MAX_SETTLEMENT_FEE_0_DAYS) -> warp past maturity -> take -> assert settlement fee > 0. Invariant tested: post-maturity settlement fee must equal the 0d breakpoint value. Scoped impact: fee inconsistency; lenders receive less than expected post-maturity. Proof idea: unit test asserting settlementFee(id, 0) == marketSettlementFee(id, 0) post-maturity.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit an Authorization where authorization.authorizer is a smart contract that has authorized ecrecoverAuthorizer in Midnight, and the signer is a different address that is authorized by the contract in Midnight, creating a two-hop delegation (contract -> signer -> ecrecoverAuthorizer) that grants authorization on behalf of the contract? Preconditions: contract C has authorized ecrecoverAuthorizer in Midnight; C has authorized signer S in Midnight; S signs Authorization({authorizer: C, authorized: attacker}). Call sequence: midnight.setIsAuthorized(ecrecoverAuthorizer, true, C) -> midnight.setIsAuthorized(S, true, C) -> S signs auth -> ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth, S_sig) -> check isAuthorized(C, S) -> passes. Invariant tested: delegation via authorized signers must be correctly validated. Scoped impact: gate bypass via multi-hop delegation. Proof idea: unit test asserting two-hop delegation works as designed; verify no unintended escalation.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: Midnight.setDefaultSettlementFee] Can the feeSetter set defaultSettlementFee[loanToken][i] to a value that is a valid multiple of CBP but exceeds maxSettlementFee(i), and does the SettlementFeeTooHigh check correctly prevent this for both default and market fee setters? Preconditions: feeSetter calls setDefaultSettlementFee(token, i, maxSettlementFee(i) + CBP). Call sequence: setDefaultSettlementFee(token, 0, MAX_SETTLEMENT_FEE_0_DAYS + CBP) -> should revert with SettlementFeeTooHigh. Invariant tested: settlement fees must be bounded by per-index maximums. Scoped impact: fee inconsistency if bounds are bypassable. Proof idea: fuzz over fee values near maxSettlementFee(i); assert revert for values exceeding cap.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized + Midnight.multicall] Can an attacker use Midnight.multicall to atomically change the feeSetter role and then set market fees in the same transaction, creating a state where market fees are set by an address that was only transiently the feeSetter, and does this violate the role-controlled parameter invariant? Preconditions: attacker is roleSetter; attacker wants to set market fees without being permanently feeSetter. Call sequence: multicall([setFeeSetter(attacker), setMarketSettlementFee(id, i, fee), setFeeSetter(originalFeeSetter)]). Invariant tested: role-controlled parameters must only be set by the designated role holder at the time of the call. Scoped impact: role-controlled parameter inconsistency via transient role assignment. Proof idea: multicall test asserting transient feeSetter can set market fees; verify this is by-design or a bug.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: Midnight.setMarketSettlementFee + Midnight.settlementFee] Can the feeSetter set all 7 settlement fee breakpoints to 0 on a created market, causing settlementFee(id, ttm) to return 0 for all TTM values, and does this create a state inconsistency where the market has zero settlement fees while claimableSettlementFee[token] never increases, potentially causing accounting desync if the fee was previously non-zero? Preconditions: market created with non-zero default fees; feeSetter sets all breakpoints to 0. Call sequence: touchMarket -> setMarketSettlementFee(id, i, 0) for i in 0..6 -> take -> assert claimableSettlementFee[token] unchanged. Invariant tested: claimableSettlementFee must equal sum of (buyerAssets - sellerAssets) across all takes. Scoped impact: fee accounting inconsistency; protocol loses fee revenue. Proof idea: invariant test asserting claimableSettlementFee increases by exactly buyerAssets - sellerAssets per take.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker exploit the fact that the AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH does not include a market ID or any market-specific parameter, allowing a signed Authorization to be used to grant authorization that affects operations across all markets, not just a specific market? Preconditions: authorizer signs Authorization granting attacker authorization; attacker uses this to perform operations on any market. Call sequence: ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth) -> midnight.isAuthorized[authorizer][attacker] = true -> attacker calls withdraw/repay/withdrawCollateral on any market on behalf of authorizer. Invariant tested: authorization must be scoped appropriately; cross-market authorization must be intentional. Scoped impact: unauthorized seizure/withdrawal across all markets. Proof idea: unit test asserting authorized attacker can operate on any market; verify this is by-design.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: Midnight.setMarketContinuousFee + Midnight.claimContinuousFee] Can the feeClaimer call claimContinuousFee with amount > continuousFeeCredit, causing an underflow in _marketState.continuousFeeCredit (which is uint128), and does this create a state inconsistency where continuousFeeCredit wraps around to a large value? Preconditions: market has continuousFeeCredit = X; feeClaimer calls claimContinuousFee(market, X+1, receiver). Call sequence: claimContinuousFee(market, continuousFeeCredit + 1, receiver) -> _marketState.continuousFeeCredit -= (X+1) -> underflow. Invariant tested: continuousFeeCredit must never underflow; claimed amount must not exceed available credit. Scoped impact: accounting corruption; solvency violation. Proof idea: fuzz over claim amounts; assert revert or correct underflow protection in Solidity 0.8.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit an Authorization with a deadline far in the future (e.g., type(uint256).max), creating a long-lived authorization that remains valid indefinitely, and does the protocol provide any mechanism to invalidate such authorizations other than incrementing the nonce? Preconditions: authorizer signs Authorization with deadline = type(uint256).max. Call sequence: ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth_with_max_deadline) -> authorization valid indefinitely. Invariant tested: long-lived authorizations must be revocable; nonce increment must be the only invalidation mechanism. Scoped impact: gate bypass if authorizer cannot revoke; fund freeze if revocation is blocked. Proof idea: unit test asserting authorization with max deadline is accepted; verify revocation via nonce increment works.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: Midnight.setMarketSettlementFee + Midnight.take] Can the feeSetter change marketSettlementFee mid-take (not possible in a single tx, but via front-running), causing a taker to pay a different settlement fee than they expected when they signed their transaction, and does the protocol provide any atomic price-check mechanism? Preconditions: taker submits take transaction; feeSetter front-runs with setMarketSettlementFee increasing fees. Call sequence: feeSetter calls setMarketSettlementFee(id, i, higherFee) -> taker's take executes with higher fee -> taker pays more than expected. Invariant tested: takers must be able to atomically verify settlement fees before taking. Scoped impact: direct loss to taker via unexpected fee increase. Proof idea: simulation test with fee change between signing and execution; verify NatSpec comment about smart-contract price checks.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker who has obtained isAuthorized[victim][attacker] == true (via EcrecoverAuthorizer) use this to call midnight.setIsAuthorized(ecrecoverAuthorizer, false, victim), revoking the victim's ability to use EcrecoverAuthorizer for future authorizations, creating a DoS on the victim's authorization flow? Preconditions: attacker is authorized by victim; victim has authorized ecrecoverAuthorizer. Call sequence: attacker calls midnight.setIsAuthorized(ecrecoverAuthorizer, false, victim) -> victim can no longer use ecrecoverAuthorizer. Invariant tested: authorized agents must not be able to revoke the authorizer's own tools. Scoped impact: DoS on victim's authorization flow; fund freeze. Proof idea: unit test asserting authorized attacker can revoke ecrecoverAuthorizer permission; verify this is a known risk.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: Midnight.setDefaultSettlementFee + Midnight.touchMarket] Can an attacker create two markets with the same loanToken but different collateral parameters, where both markets inherit the same defaultSettlementFee at creation time, and then the feeSetter sets different market-specific fees for each, creating a state where two markets for the same loanToken have divergent fee schedules? Preconditions: two markets with same loanToken but different collateral params; feeSetter sets different fees for each. Call sequence: touchMarket(marketA) -> touchMarket(marketB) -> setMarketSettlementFee(idA, i, feeA) -> setMarketSettlementFee(idB, i, feeB). Invariant tested: per-market fees are independent; same loanToken markets can have different fees. Scoped impact: fee inconsistency between markets; takers may prefer one market over another. Proof idea: state-transition test asserting independent fee storage per market ID.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker submit an Authorization where the AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH is computed incorrectly (e.g., using a different field ordering), causing the digest to differ from what the signer intended, and does the hardcoded AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH constant correctly match the actual struct encoding? Preconditions: AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH = keccak256('Authorization(address authorizer,address authorized,bool isAuthorized,uint256 nonce,uint256 deadline)'). Call sequence: verify AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH == keccak256(AUTHORIZATION_TYPE) in test. Invariant tested: EIP-712 type hash must exactly match the struct definition. Scoped impact: signature bypass if type hash is incorrect. Proof idea: unit test asserting AUTHORIZATION_TYPEHASH == keccak256(AUTHORIZATION_TYPE) as in SetIsAuthorizedWithSigTest.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: Midnight.setMarketTickSpacing + Midnight.setMarketSettlementFee] Can the tickSpacingSetter reduce tick spacing on a market while the feeSetter simultaneously changes settlement fees, creating a state where the market has both a new tick spacing and new fees applied atomically via multicall, and does this create any inconsistency in the market state? Preconditions: market created; tickSpacingSetter and feeSetter are the same address or use multicall. Call sequence: multicall([setMarketTickSpacing(id, 1), setMarketSettlementFee(id, 0, maxFee)]) -> both changes applied atomically. Invariant tested: simultaneous role-controlled parameter changes must not create inconsistent market state. Scoped impact: state inconsistency in market parameters. Proof idea: multicall test asserting both changes are applied correctly and independently.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker exploit the fact that EcrecoverAuthorizer.nonce is per-authorizer (not per-authorizer-authorized pair), meaning a single nonce sequence covers all possible (authorized, isAuthorized) combinations, and submitting any Authorization for an authorizer increments the nonce for ALL future authorizations by that authorizer, potentially blocking specific intended authorizations? Preconditions: authorizer has signed auth A (nonce=0, authorized=X) and auth B (nonce=1, authorized=Y); attacker submits auth B first. Call sequence: attacker submits auth B (nonce=1) -> InvalidNonce (nonce is 0) -> attacker submits auth A (nonce=0) -> nonce becomes 1 -> auth B (nonce=1) can now be submitted. Invariant tested: nonce ordering must be strictly sequential; out-of-order submission must be impossible. Scoped impact: DoS on specific authorization if attacker can force nonce advancement. Proof idea: fuzz over submission ordering; assert only sequential nonce submissions succeed.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium State inconsistency between created and uncreated markets, default fees and market fees, or role-controlled parameters'] [Function: Midnight.setDefaultSettlementFee] Can the feeSetter set defaultSettlementFee[loanToken][i] to a value that is not a multiple of CBP (1e12), and does the FeeNotMultipleOfFeeCbp check correctly prevent this for both setDefaultSettlementFee and setMarketSettlementFee? Preconditions: feeSetter calls setDefaultSettlementFee(token, i, CBP - 1). Call sequence: setDefaultSettlementFee(token, 0, CBP - 1) -> should revert with FeeNotMultipleOfFeeCbp. Invariant tested: all fee values must be multiples of CBP to fit in uint16 cbp representation. Scoped impact: fee accounting inconsistency if non-CBP-multiple fees are stored. Proof idea: fuzz over fee values not divisible by CBP; assert revert.
